title: Progress Claims, Retention, and the July Cash Wall in Construction
excerpt: Construction businesses work flat out in June and still hit a cash wall in July. The reason is structural: progress claims, retention holdbacks, and the super due date combine in a predictable squeeze that visibility alone can prevent.
content:
June is one of the most active months on construction sites across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria. End-of-financial-year deadlines push principals to chase completions. Head contractors are running hard to close out milestones. Specialist trades and subcontractors carry full books and extended rosters to get through the work before June 30. By the end of the month, the job sheets look impressive and the revenue tracker shows strong numbers.

Then July arrives. For many builders, subcontractors, and trades businesses, the bank position in the first three weeks of the new financial year looks materially worse than that June revenue figure suggested it would. The work was done. The invoices are raised. The cash is somewhere in the billing cycle, not yet in the account.

This is not a bookkeeping error or a run of bad luck. It is the structural reality of how construction businesses get paid, and the businesses that manage it well understand the timing gap before it opens rather than after.

The Progress Claim Cycle

Construction revenue does not land the way most other business revenue does. A professional services firm invoices at month end and collects within thirty days. A hospitality business takes cash or card at the table. A construction business bills on progress: claim submitted, assessment period, certification, payment terms. Each step takes time, and the cumulative delay concentrates the cash shortfall in the months that follow the busiest billing period.

A progress claim submitted in the final days of June under a standard AS 4000 or ABIC commercial contract typically faces an assessment window of ten to fifteen business days, followed by payment at the end of the applicable payment period. Depending on how the contract is structured, that cash can arrive anywhere from mid-July to late August. The work was performed in June. The revenue sits in the accounts receivable ledger. The cash comes well into the new financial year.

The Security of Payment Act, which applies in Queensland and NSW with state-specific provisions, gives subcontractors genuine protections for their right to be paid on time. It does not shorten the payment cycle. A subcontractor following the process correctly and in full can still see their June labour legitimately converted to August cash. The right to payment and the timing of cash receipt are two different things, and conflating them is where cash plans tend to go wrong.

What Retention Does to Working Capital

Beyond payment timing, most commercial construction contracts include a retention clause. Typically five percent of each progress claim is withheld by the head contractor or developer until practical completion, and sometimes held further into a defects liability period after that. For a subcontractor running a $1.5 million package, that is $75,000 in earned cash tied to a project milestone that may be months away.

Across several concurrent projects, a well-established trades business can accumulate $300,000 to $400,000 or more in retention claims that are legally owed, completely earned, and operationally inaccessible. The money is real. The work is done. The cash cannot be deployed.

A

>Working Capital Unlock engagement maps exactly where the cash is locked: debtors outstanding across the progress claim cycle, retention held by head contractors across active projects, materials suppliers with terms that can be extended to offset the gap, and any facility headroom that can bridge the window. The typical finding for a construction business is that the working capital constraint is a timing problem rather than a profitability problem. The money exists. It is simply not yet liquid.

The July Pressure Stack

The payment timing gap becomes a genuine problem when it meets the obligations that arrive in early July regardless of what the billing cycle is doing.

Superannuation for the April to June quarter is due by 28 July. For a building business with a team of eight to fifteen people running a full June roster, that super liability is a material outgoing with a fixed date. The wages that drove the month's productivity were paid in June. The superannuation on those wages falls due in July, at exactly the point the June progress claims are still in assessment and the retention pool has not moved.

Materials suppliers who extended goodwill through the final sprint expect settlement in July. Plant hire, formwork, concrete, and steel invoices from the last push before June 30 come due in the same window. And if new contracts are mobilising in July, as they often do when projects handed over on June 30 clear site for the next phase, the forward working capital demand starts building again before the prior month's cash has arrived.

The result is a business that was genuinely productive in June, has real revenue on the ledger, and still finds itself managing a tighter cash position than the activity level suggested it should have. This is the July cash wall. It is predictable, structural, and manageable, but only when the picture is visible before the month begins.

Building the Forward View Before June 30

The discipline that changes the pattern is visibility ahead of the squeeze. A

>13-week cash flow forecast built in the last two weeks of June, one that maps the expected collection dates on all outstanding progress claims, the retention positions across current projects, the super and supplier obligations due in July, and any forward mobilisation commitments, gives a construction business owner the lead time to act before the pressure arrives rather than after it has already opened.

Acting might mean negotiating an extended term with a materials supplier. Scheduling a direct conversation with a head contractor about a certification that has gone quiet. Drawing on a facility that is available but unused. Or simply knowing in late June that July will be tight and making deliberate decisions about discretionary spending before the window closes.

ProfitPulse works with construction and trades businesses through exactly this part of the calendar, building the cash visibility that turns the annual July squeeze from an annual surprise into a planned event. If June is running hard and the July cash picture is not yet clear, that picture is worth building now.

faqs.q: How does a progress claim work and when do builders actually get paid?
faqs.a: Construction billing is structured around progress claims rather than standard invoices. When a milestone or payment period is reached, the contractor submits a claim to the head contractor or developer. The recipient has a set period, typically ten to fifteen business days under standard commercial contracts, to assess and certify the amount. Payment then follows within the contract's agreed terms. In practice, work completed in June is commonly paid in late July or August depending on how the contract is structured and how quickly the certification moves through the head contractor's process.

faqs.q: What is retention money in construction and how does it affect cash flow?
faqs.a: Retention is a percentage of each progress claim, typically five percent, withheld by the head contractor or developer as security against defective work. It is held until practical completion and sometimes through a subsequent defects liability period. For subcontractors and builders running several concurrent projects, the cumulative retention balance can represent several hundred thousand dollars in cash that is earned but inaccessible. A working capital review maps the total retention position across all active contracts and identifies where recovery can be accelerated.

faqs.q: Why does July feel harder for construction businesses even after a productive June?
faqs.a: Because the cash from June's work has not arrived when the July obligations fall due. Progress claim payments follow a thirty-to-sixty-day cycle after submission, which means June revenue often lands in late July or August. At the same time, the April to June superannuation is due by 28 July, materials suppliers expect settlement after the June push, and new project mobilisations create forward working capital demands. The business was productive in June; the bank position in July reflects that productivity with a delay the month-end P&L does not show.

faqs.q: What does the Security of Payment Act do for subcontractors in Queensland?
faqs.a: Queensland's Building Industry Fairness (Security of Payment) Act gives subcontractors and contractors the right to make payment claims and pursue adjudication if claims are not paid or are disputed within the statutory timeframes. It sets maximum windows for payment responses and adjudication decisions and provides real legal protection for the right to be paid. It does not change the underlying cash timing cycle. A subcontractor who pursues their entitlement successfully through the process can still face a six-to-eight-week gap between completing the work and receiving the cash.

faqs.q: How should a construction or trades business forecast cash flow around EOFY?
faqs.a: Build a thirteen-week cash flow forecast in the last two weeks of June that maps every outstanding progress claim by its expected assessment and payment date, the retention balance across all active projects, the superannuation liability due by 28 July, and the forward mobilisation costs on contracts starting in the new financial year. Updated weekly through July, this model gives the business owner six to eight weeks of lead time to act on any gap before it becomes a problem. ProfitPulse builds these forecasts for construction and trades businesses across Queensland and NSW.

faqs.q: What working capital options help construction businesses with tied-up retention?
faqs.a: Several approaches improve the position. Retention bonds, where an insurance instrument replaces the cash holding, are accepted by some head contractors and developers and free the retained funds immediately. Invoice financing against certified but unpaid progress claims provides a bridge across the payment timing gap. Extending supplier payment terms through a structured payables programme reduces the cash demand in the same window the progress claims are settling. A combination mapped against the specific contract and supplier landscape is typically more effective than any single measure.

faqs.q: When should a building or trades business owner consider working with a fractional CFO?
faqs.a: For builders and trades businesses growing beyond five to eight million in revenue, where project cash flow complexity, retention management across multiple sites, and working capital planning require more than a quarterly meeting with an accountant, a fractional CFO arrangement provides the financial oversight without the cost of a full-time hire. The focus typically covers job-level profitability tracking, progress claim optimisation, and the forward cash view that prevents the July squeeze from catching the business unprepared year after year.
